course_description:
COMU7301 examines strategic communication theories and best practice frameworks at a postgraduate level. Students are asked to develop research-based communication campaigns that aim to be ethical, socially aware and impactful. Weekly lectures and tutorials explore relevant theories, research methods and planning techniques that will be applied to real life communication problems. During tutorials students will develop the skills necessary to design strategic communication/public relations campaigns for a real life client. You will actively plan and mobilise various strategies and tactics to build relations, reputation and community. The aim is to communicate the client's message successfully to key publics through online and offline communication channels. The ultimate goal is to build and maintain meaningful and truthful multi stakeholder relationships in the context of fast moving and increasingly complex societies.

COMU7301 will provide you with the knowledge, tools and capabilities necessary to design evidence-based strategic communication campaigns and projects for public, private, and third sector organisations. You will learn to identify, select and apply the best communication strategies and tactics to help a real-life clientﾠbuild, maintain,ﾠ and manage effective and ethical stakeholder relationships and brand reputation. You will study, research, and design a strategic communication plan that includes, (a) researching the environment ﾠin which organisations and their audiences operate; (b) understanding and analysing the culture, habits and tastes of relevant publics; (c) selecting the best communication platforms, outletsﾠ and tools to communicate with those publics using compelling, socially aware,ﾠand meaningful messages. Overall, you will become an accomplished,ﾠcourageous, and respectful scholar and professional, a fully connected and culturally capable global citizen, and an influential professional communicator, consistent with all UQ graduate attributes.ﾠ
timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: COMU7301 aims to equip students with a robust understanding of strategic communication practice with special focus on the development of evidence-based campaigns and projects. Research-based communication is essential in an era defined by public participation, traditional and emerging media communication technologies, the circulation of data, and the importance of fact checking in increasingly complex settings. You will critically explore discipline specific theories and research methods and professional frameworks for planning and evaluation that will equip you with the necessary skills to build and manage relations and communicate strategically, ethically, respectfully, and effectively with key stakeholder groups.

learning_outcomes.description: Explore, discuss, and apply theoretical and professional frameworks to real life communication cases, situations or problems; demonstrate knowledge of ethical and regulatory obligations linked with communication planning; and provide critical insights into contemporary trends, challenges and opportunities in the strategic communication/PR field and practice.

Develop problem solving capabilities to work individually and in groups with integrity, vision and courage; analyse a client brief, its aims, context and challenges; specific communication needs, and main stakeholders involved; and conduct feasible research methods whose findings will inform the campaign planning process.

Design meaningful, ethical, and effective strategic communication campaigns, programs or projects that propose: (a) clear goals and a holistic strategy; (b) 'SMART' objectives; (c) meaningful messaging; (d) culturally aware strategies and tactics to connect with relevant publics via interpersonal, community and digital communication techniques, tools and channels; and, (e) adequate evaluation methods that ensure that objectives are met.

Analyse, write, and present cases, projects and campaigns critically, strategically, and ethically by effectively researching and using library, digital, and industry resources; academic referencing styles; relevant rubrics and frameworks; and basic statistical information and/or data analytics.

assessment_summary.due_date: 24/03/2026 - 26/05/2026; Task sheets will be written during lecture time.

assessment_summary.due_date: 11/06/2026 4:00 pm; Submit via Blackboard the written Campaign Plan on 11 June.; Oral Pitch: Your group will pitch this plan in tutorials in week 13 (values 8 pts of the overall grade = 20% of 40 marked using the A4 rubric). You may bring one or two slides to the class. Groups will have 8 to 10 minutes per group to present.

assessment_details.title: In-Class Writing Task (sequence)
assessment_details.due_date: 24/03/2026 - 26/05/2026 Task sheets will be written during lecture time.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: There will be 8 in-class writing tasks held in the lecture beginning in week 5 and concluding in week 13 (no writing tasks in week 8 due to industry guest lecture). This assignment asks students to demonstrate knowledge and understanding of some of the main strategic communication theories, concepts and professional themes discussed during the lectures in the previous weeks.You will be asked to briefly define and critically reflect on the topic or concept in your own words, in a handwritten task-sheet. You will be prepared to explain why the concept/topic is important to understand, or resolve, strategic communication projects in either industry or academic settings. You will also be prepared to link the concept to a 'real-life' example from lectures, tutorials, weekly readings and/or reliable media sites . You will be given 15 minutes (5 minutes to prepare and 10 mins to write) to provide a handwritten answer of a maximum of 250 words (5 X 250 = 1250 words in total ). A list of key topics/concepts will be published on Blackboard (Assessment and Learning Resources section) in week 2. You are expected to acknowledge or cite some of the ideas or authors that were discussed in previous classes or in the material you studied for the assignment . Therefore, reading, understanding and applying weekly material and participating in discussions in lectures and tutorials will be crucial to obtain the maximum grade. You may use an 'argument map' to develop your answer. This assessment evaluates students' own critical thinking and problem solving capabilities, engagement with course themes, and academic knowledge . More information about this assignment, list of main topics, and the rubric, will be posted to Blackboard in week 2. IMPORTANT NOTE 1 : There will be 8 opportunities to take the In-Class Writing Tasks, but you only need to take 5 (valued at 6 pts each), which together will form a maximum of 30 pts . I f you do more than 5 Writing Tasks, we will select the best 5 . This also means that you may miss up to 3 tasks without penalty. However, there will be NO opportunities to re-take individual papers, and no alternative assessment will be available (except in very specific circumstances , such as a ‘Disability Action Plan’ in place, or other genuine, well documented/supported reasons, such as medical issues, carer’s responsibility; sudden accident) . So please, try to take as many as In-Class Writing Tasks as you can. IMPORTANT NOTE 2 : This assignment will be completed in person, during lectures, and is handwritten. You must bring your own pen(s) to the lecture. We will provide a task-sheet (paper) where you will write your name, student number, tutorial group and/or tutor’s name, and your answer. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. So please engage with concepts, relevant authors, and themes from the beginning. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted . Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This assignment will be completed in person, during lectures, and is handwritten. There will be 8 in-class writing tasks held in the lecture beginning in week 5 and concluding in week 13. You will be given 15 minutes (5 minutes to prepare and 10 mins to write) to provide a handwritten 250-word answer (maximum). A task sheet (paper) will be provided in the lecture. You must bring your own pen(s). You will write your names, student number, tutorial number/day/time, and tutor's name. At the end of the 15 minutes, you will hand the paper with your answer to the lecturer and/or members of the teaching team who are present in the lecture room. Writing Tasks will not be set on holidays, or when some special guests are scheduled, or in the case of unexpected events. Please refer to Blackboard for dates.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
This assessment task will be completed in person. Students will NOT be able to apply for an extension via the Unitask portal for the presentation. Please contact your tutor/course coordinator directly to see if alternative arrangements are available (provided you have well supported reasons).
Late submission
Late Submission is NOT APPLICABLE (see assignment instructions).

assessment_details.title: Situation Analysis & Research Proposal
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assignment (and the following ones) will be developed in groups of ideally 4 members (max 5) with whom you will continue to work for assignments 2 and 3 . If you wish to work individually you will need to talk with the course coordinator and present genuine and well supported reasons. The groups will send an email to the tutor and course coordinator with the full names of all team members and the name of the selected client. You will also write your names on a spread sheet that will be provided in tutorials. Teamwork involves full commitment to work with professionalism, executing allocated tasks in time, and respecting each other (see UQ group work guidelines and policy ; also material on Bb). IMPORTANT: ALL students will self-enrol in a Blackboard group (instructions Bb). If you do not self enrol on Bb, you will not be able to submit or see your feedback and grades. Talk with the course coordinator and tutor if you have any issues in this regard. The team will submit just one report (the same for all) via Blackboard, and will receive the same feedback and marks. You may use a power point or word document to present this task. It may written using a mix of narrative style with dot points. This task should include a graph/table of your SWOT, PEST, or other type of situation or contextual analysis as an illustration of your initial research work. To strengthen your situation analysis you may also include a graph with a communication audit and preliminary stakeholder map (optional but desirable). You are expected to include a reference list citing any material or secondary research used in the findings, e.g. reports, textbook frameworks (the ref. list will not included in the word count). The task consists of two parts, (1) Written Report (5% - 500 words aprox.) It will include: i. Key elements of your client organisation, background, ‘the issue’, that is, client’s communication need and aim ('problem statement') (presented by the client in week 3). ii. A Situation Analysis and/or PEST analysis, and a communication/media audit of your client organisation (this is preliminary background desk research). iii. Identify the main communication problems/opportunities/challenges that your client organisation faces. iv. Identify client's key stakeholder groups for this campaign specifically (stakeholder map may be included). (2) Written Research Proposal (5% - 500 words approx.) It will include: i. Research proposal : Your research approach and methods will include a sample research item (e.g. type of secondary research; or a questionnaire for a survey; or a type of social media analysis; or other. ii. The proposal should consist of feasible and realistic primary (quantitative or qualitative), and/OR secondary research methods; and/or social media analysis; and/or any mix of the above. Single methods are possible. iii. Justify briefly why the method(s) proposed is/are appropriate to respond to the client's brief and target publics's attitudes, in the context in which they operate . iv. Research schedule: A realistic timeline to complete the research task whose findings will inform A3. Feedback from your markers will help you advance towards the next step. More informationon Blackboard (Course Resources and Assessment folders). This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Group submission via Blackboard
Blackboard Assignments
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Assignments. Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Important submission information
You must click on the Submit button to submit your work for assessment. Academic staff will not see the files if you have merely saved them as a submission in progress. Files that are submitted CANNOT be retrieved for editing and re-submission. Once submitted, they are delivered to the Course Coordinator. It is your responsibility to check the Submission History and confirm your assignment was successfully submitted. To allow for the many possible technical problems with computers and the internet, students are advised to commence assignment uploads at least 3 hours before they are due. If you don’t receive a submission ID, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. It is your responsibility to check the assignment preview and confirm that the assignment has been successfully submitted. Students should take a screenshot showing the successful submission to confirm that they have followed the correct process.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your Blackboard assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
COMU7301 assessment is incremental. Assignments build upon each other. This assignment's results will help students proceed to conduct research for assignment 3. Fast turnaround time is important to allow time to conduct the research.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Research Findings Report
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This third task, a Report, will continue to be prepared and submitted with your respective groups. After conducting the research proposed in assignment 2, A3 asks you to prepare a Report that will include: (a) A list of research findings; (b) An outline that will explain and justify why and how those findings will help you design the strategic communication plan for your client, and will enable you to frame, reframe, or refine the goal; main message(s); target publics; (c) A minimum of one, preferably two preliminary SMART campaign objectives; (d) A graph, table, or other schematic material whereby your group will display your research findings to your client and marker. This means that the Report will include some form of visualisation of your findings (you can use simple tables, graphs of any type, or other visuals). Your report should have professional quality and creativity. For this assignment you will try to use the organisation's logo, colours, and overall look and feel (see style guide if available). Also, you should start creating your own agency/consultancy name and logo to receive feedback (this is a simulation, but it is important to be and look profesional). Through this task and its Report students will demonstrate their understanding of the communication challenge or problem presented by the client. Students are expected to identify and reflect upon the importance of their research findings and the way those findings will inform their campaign plan (A4). Students will also demonstrate their problem solving capabilities in strategic communication projects or issues. IMPORTANT NOTE 1 : For this report you will NOT include strategies and tactics YET (these will be included in the final Campaign Plan = A4). IMPORTANT NOTE 2: You are expected to include a reference list citing any material or secondary research used in the findings, e.g. textbook material, relevant reports, statistics (this ref. list will not included in the word count). This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Strategic Communication Campaign Plan
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Presentation, Product/ Design
assessment_details.due_date: 11/06/2026 4:00 pm Submit via Blackboard the written Campaign Plan on 11 June. Oral Pitch: Your group will pitch this plan in tutorials in week 13 (values 8 pts of the overall grade = 20% of 40 marked using the A4 rubric). You may bring one or two slides to the class. Groups will have 8 to 10 minutes per group to present.
assessment_details.task_description: The fourth and final piece assessment, the Campaign Plan, consists of your Plan with the full proposal responding to the client. This task will be prepared, delivered and submitted by your group in two formats: (a) Pitch in Class on week 13 in tutorials (8 pts = 20%), listing the main elements of the campaign and highlighting a "star tactic"; and (b) Campaign Plan written and presented in slides submitted by the group via Blackboard (32 pts = 80%) on the due date (during Exam week 1). The Pitch: In week 13 you will orally pitch the main elements of your plan and will display (in writing, digitally, graphically, audio-visually) ONE of the key tactics, assets, or collaterals that you will propose in your plan (the most important). Your group will have between 8 to10 mins including feedback. For example, a media release; a social media tactic; a website; a newsletter; brochure or flyer; a blog post for the client’s website; social media material; podcast or video; a PR event; a speech/conference; advocacy/lobby campaign; fundraising event; community relations/engagement plan and/or event, etc. In your pitch you will briefly justify why this is your main tactic, and how it will contribute to the success of your campaign for your client. The tactics and "star tactic' should aim to be effective, ethical, socially and contextually aware, displaying attractive and meaningful content that can potentially meet the client's communication need. This task values 8 pts (20% of 40, which is the overall grade for this assignment) which will be marked using the overall assignment rubric ('Plan/Design' item). Your pitch, will last between 8 and 10 mins and will be recorded for marking purposes. Please let your course coordinator and tutor know in writing if you would prefer not to be recorded by either voice or image so accomodations can be made. Reference any computational material used in your "star" tactic or other elements. You may bring 1-3 slides to pitch your plan in class. All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device). Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: Moderation of marking; Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or Re-marking following a successful re-mark application. Your Campaign Plan ideally written in presentation slides format will include: Your consultancy name, professional-looking logo, and 'credentials' as PR/Comm consultant(s), and a cover letter addressed to your client (optional). Executive Summary with the main campaign elements proposed by your group. Your complete Campaign Plan where you will deploy: A refined/revised version of some of the elements already submitted for assignments 2 and 3, such as, (a) client's communication need/issue; (b) outline of research methods used and main findings; (c) main goal (intended outcome) of the specific campaign; (d) strategic approach; (e) key publics, (f) main message(s); (g) complete list of SMART objectives; and the following. New elements: Communication/action strategies and tactics (with the complete list of tactics). Logistics: Campaign budget and schedule (two Gantt charts table or other). Proposed evaluation methods (which may be used in the future, after execution) that will demonstrate that the objectives will be effectively met; ideally in a table vis-a-vis target publics and strategies. Conclusion. A paragraph where you will briefly deploy the rationale, limitations, theoretical considerations (i.e. theoretical material that helped/inspired you in the planning process), used to inform the campaign proposal; and possible ethical/legal considerations that may arise from, or concern the campaign plan (if any) according to best practice frameworks ; final recommendations (if any). A reference list of any theories, frameworks, statistics from textbooks, reports, or online resources used in the planning, design and illustration of the Campaign, and other elements that may or may not be computational (e.g. the use of Canva, or other) which must be cited for integrity/ethical reasons (the ref. list will not be included in the word count). You will also reference your client's company or website. Both, the Campaign Plan content and slides must aim at high a level of professional quality, creativity, and authenticity. All the elements in the Plan must respond to the client brief and campaign goal, and must be consistent with the client organisation's style guide . You will include the budget, schedule and evaluation methods (simulation) using tables/graphs for some of those items. Note that human writing and visuals are preferred to AI writing or images, which may look boring, repetitive and often lack creativity. You must not mention the client and its issues if you intend to use AI tools (if so you must reference and acknowledge its use). Overall, this is the task that will demonstrate your group's critical thinking and true problem solving capabilities, which are highly appreciated by the industry. It is crucial that students take relevant MaPS training workshops, as well as Library and LinkedIn Learning workshops in advance, during the first weeks of the semester. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Group work via Blackboard
Blackboard Assignments
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Assignments. Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Important submission information
You must click on the Submit button to submit your work for assessment. Academic staff will not see the files if you have merely saved them as a submission in progress. Files that are submitted CANNOT be retrieved for editing and re-submission. Once submitted, they are delivered to the Course Coordinator. It is your responsibility to check the Submission History and confirm your assignment was successfully submitted. To allow for the many possible technical problems with computers and the internet, students are advised to commence assignment uploads at least 3 hours before they are due. If you don’t receive a submission ID, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. It is your responsibility to check the assignment preview and confirm that the assignment has been successfully submitted. Students should take a screenshot showing the successful submission to confirm that they have followed the correct process.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your Blackboard assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note, extensions are available for the written campaign only. If students are unable to attend the in-class pitches in Week 13, they are to contact the course coordinator and/or tutor as soon as possible to discuss their circumstances and options.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.ai_statement:
The fourth and final piece assessment, the Campaign Plan, consists of your Plan with the full proposal responding to the client. This task will be prepared, delivered and submitted by your group in two formats: (a) Pitch in Class on week 13 in tutorials (8 pts = 20%), listing the main elements of the campaign and highlighting a "star tactic"; and (b) Campaign Plan written and presented in slides submitted by the group via Blackboard (32 pts = 80%) on the due date (during Exam week 1). The Pitch: In week 13 you will orally pitch the main elements of your plan and will display (in writing, digitally, graphically, audio-visually) ONE of the key tactics, assets, or collaterals that you will propose in your plan (the most important). Your group will have between 8 to10 mins including feedback. For example, a media release; a social media tactic; a website; a newsletter; brochure or flyer; a blog post for the client’s website; social media material; podcast or video; a PR event; a speech/conference; advocacy/lobby campaign; fundraising event; community relations/engagement plan and/or event, etc. In your pitch you will briefly justify why this is your main tactic, and how it will contribute to the success of your campaign for your client. The tactics and "star tactic' should aim to be effective, ethical, socially and contextually aware, displaying attractive and meaningful content that can potentially meet the client's communication need. This task values 8 pts (20% of 40, which is the overall grade for this assignment) which will be marked using the overall assignment rubric ('Plan/Design' item). Your pitch, will last between 8 and 10 mins and will be recorded for marking purposes. Please let your course coordinator and tutor know in writing if you would prefer not to be recorded by either voice or image so accomodations can be made. Reference any computational material used in your "star" tactic or other elements. You may bring 1-3 slides to pitch your plan in class. All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device). Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: Moderation of marking; Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or Re-marking following a successful re-mark application. Your Campaign Plan ideally written in presentation slides format will include: Your consultancy name, professional-looking logo, and 'credentials' as PR/Comm consultant(s), and a cover letter addressed to your client (optional). Executive Summary with the main campaign elements proposed by your group. Your complete Campaign Plan where you will deploy: A refined/revised version of some of the elements already submitted for assignments 2 and 3, such as, (a) client's communication need/issue; (b) outline of research methods used and main findings; (c) main goal (intended outcome) of the specific campaign; (d) strategic approach; (e) key publics, (f) main message(s); (g) complete list of SMART objectives; and the following. New elements: Communication/action strategies and tactics (with the complete list of tactics). Logistics: Campaign budget and schedule (two Gantt charts table or other). Proposed evaluation methods (which may be used in the future, after execution) that will demonstrate that the objectives will be effectively met; ideally in a table vis-a-vis target publics and strategies. Conclusion. A paragraph where you will briefly deploy the rationale, limitations, theoretical considerations (i.e. theoretical material that helped/inspired you in the planning process), used to inform the campaign proposal; and possible ethical/legal considerations that may arise from, or concern the campaign plan (if any) according to best practice frameworks ; final recommendations (if any). A reference list of any theories, frameworks, statistics from textbooks, reports, or online resources used in the planning, design and illustration of the Campaign, and other elements that may or may not be computational (e.g. the use of Canva, or other) which must be cited for integrity/ethical reasons (the ref. list will not be included in the word count). You will also reference your client's company or website. Both, the Campaign Plan content and slides must aim at high a level of professional quality, creativity, and authenticity. All the elements in the Plan must respond to the client brief and campaign goal, and must be consistent with the client organisation's style guide . You will include the budget, schedule and evaluation methods (simulation) using tables/graphs for some of those items. Note that human writing and visuals are preferred to AI writing or images, which may look boring, repetitive and often lack creativity. You must not mention the client and its issues if you intend to use AI tools (if so you must reference and acknowledge its use). Overall, this is the task that will demonstrate your group's critical thinking and true problem solving capabilities, which are highly appreciated by the industry. It is crucial that students take relevant MaPS training workshops, as well as Library and LinkedIn Learning workshops in advance, during the first weeks of the semester. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Lecture: Welcome and Introduction Course & Assessment overview - Introduction to strategic communication theory, campaigns, and research methods. Strategic Planning "in a changing world". Best Practice Frameworks. The importance of Context. PR Fields & Ethical Practice. Meet your Librarian and MaPS team. The importance of academic integrity and writing. Learning outcomes: L01
Week 1 Turorial: NO TUTORIALS THIS WEEK Tutorials start in Week 2.

Week 2 Lecture: Theory and Practice Frameworks Continue discussing theories, concepts, theory and practice frameworks, and current topics (prepping for Assignment 1). Intro to working for a client: Listening to client briefs (which will occur in weeks 3 and 4). The importance of critical thinking: Argument Maps (Guest - Learning Designer Senior Principal Manager, Mr Chris Frost). Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 2: Turorial Introductions. Discussing the Assessment with focus on Assignment 1 (In-Class Writing Task). See case studies and examples relevant to theories and topics in recommended readings, for example, Luttrell & Cap. Chapter 11), as well as industry websites. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 3 Lecture: Theories, concepts and topics - Client Briefs (TBA) More on strategic communication theories, concepts and topics - Note that material relevant to Assignment 1 will continue throughout the semester with weekly content. Client brief 1 ( TBA ) - IMPORTANT TO ATTEND . Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 3 Tutorial Continue practicing A1: How to understand theories, concepts and topics to apply to SC/PR planning. Activities: Working with a client (role play with tutor as the client). Discussing the importance of context in strategic communication. Practising with real life examples/illustrations that can be applied to some concepts. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 4 Lecture: Planning, SA & Research - Client Briefs 2 Overview of Research Methods in strategic communication. Client briefs 2 - IMPORTANT TO ATTEND. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 4 Tutorial Working with the brief: The issue. Drafting the Problem Statement. Discussing Assignment 2, Situation Analysis and Research Proposal. Tutorial Activities: Q&As about A1 (writing tasks start the following week). Working with Client Briefs: Draft Problem Statement. Forming groups. Discussing Group work and its importance towards Assignments 2 to 4 (house rules). Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 5 Lecture: Expanding on Research Strategy & Methods - First Writing Task 1 The lecture will expand on research strategy and a variety of research methods used in strategic communication/PR (offline/online). First in-Class Writing Task (Assignment 1). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 5 Tutorial Continue prepping A2, Forming groups (final opportunity) and deciding clients. Activities: Form groups and decide their client to work on Situation Analysis and Research proposal for that client. Applying PEST and SWOT to the client's issue. Selecting some secondary (reports, statistics), and possible primary research methods (survey, interviews, textual analysis). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 6 Lecture: Continuing the Planning process - Goals, Publics, Holistic Strategy - Second in-Class Writing Task (Assignment 1) Continuing the Planning process. Goals, Publics, Holistic Strategy. Final prepping and Q&As for assignment 2 (Situation Analysis and Research Proposal due 2 April). Second in-Class Writing Task (Assignment 1). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 6: NO TUTORIALS - Study week to finalise A2 NO TUTORIALS - Study week for finalising Assignment 2.
MID-SEMESTER BREAK

Week 7 Lecture: Strategising - Third Writing Task Advancing on the planning and strategising process: SMART Objectives - Other types of Objectives. Intro to Messaging. Third in-Class Writing Task. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 7 Tutorial Workshopping goals, publics/audiences/stakeholders, and holistic strategy: Prepping for assignment 3 (conducting research and writing research findings report). Activities: Practicing SMART Objectives (targeted to stakeholders). Comparing AI Objectives with Human Objectives. Critical discussion on this issue (pros and cons). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 8 Lecture: Communication strategies and tactics Refining messaging. Introduction to Action and Communication strategies and Tactics. Q&As assignment 3. Industry Guest Lecturer on messaging and campaign strategy: Ms. Lisa Nixon, Managing Director BBS Communication. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 8 Tutorial Workshopping the Planning process (cont.): From Goals and Holistic Strategy to Messaging, Publics and SMART Objectives. Continue prepping for assignment 3, Practicing how to analyse findings and apply to the brief. Activities: Crafting the main message(s). Main elements of the campaign plan - Joining the dots. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 9 Lecture: Finalising design of Effective Strategies and Tactics - Fourth Writing Task Final Q&As A3: How to apply the findings go the campaign (A4). Using preliminary findings (if possible) to draft, for example, your main message for your campaign. Justifying why this message will be effective among the key publics - Wrapping up effective strategies, ethical and culturally aware strategies and tactics for the campaign. Possible industry guest lecturer on influencer tactics (TBA). Introduction to Logistics. Fourth In-Class Writing Task. Please attend. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 9: NO TUTORIAL - Study week to finalise A3 No tutorials this week to give your group time and space to finalise the research, and write the Research Findings Report (A3). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 10 Lecture: Logistics - Fifth In-Class Writing Task. Starting with campaign Logistics. Intro to evaluation methods (if time) Prepping for assignment 4. Last Q&As for assignment 3. Fifth In-Class Writing Task. Please attend. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 10 Tutorial Workshopping and deciding strategies and tactics for your selected clients. What are the most suitable communication tools you can use to help you client? What is your 'star' tactic? Cases, exercises. Prepping for assignment 4. Activities: Selecting strategy(ies) and some tactics. groups propose mock "star tactic" and discuss in class. Compare and display in class AI strategies (prompted by some groups in the tutorials), with human strategies, critically discuss quality and results in each case. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 11 Lecture: Finalising Logistics - Evaluation Methods 1 - The Pitch - Sixth In Class Writing Task Finalising logistics. Explaining the importance of Evaluation Methods and their types. Barcelona Principles. Selecting and presenting evaluation methods. The Pitch. Sixth In Class Writing Task. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 11 Tutorial Workshopping tactics - Prepping assignment 4. Putting together the campaign, strategies and tactics for the clients (activity). Evaluating A1 (reflective activity) Evaluating group work with your tutors (benefits and challenges). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 12 Lecture: Communication Fields & Ethical Practice - Campaign elements review - Seventh In-Class Writing Task Communication Fields & Ethical Practice - Review and final Q&As about assignment 4 and the pitch. Seventh In-Class Writing Task. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 12 Tutorial Workshopping the overall plan - Activity: Producing the best Pitch for your clients (and tutors) - The pitches will take place in tutorials in week 13. Pitching house rules. Role Play (pitching to the client as consultants). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 13 Lecture: Eighth Writing Task - Some pitches may be presented in the last lecture upon consultation - Critical reflection on learning experience Eighth WrittenTask. Please attend. Upon request, some groups/students might pitch their campaign plans during lecture time in week 13 (provided they have a well-supported reason) - Final reflection on COMU7301 Learning Experience. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 13 Tutorial: Pitching your campaign plan THE PITCH (8-10 mins per group, including feedback): Students will pitch their campaign plans and 'star' tactic ideas to receive feedback from the tutors. Bring a slide or two to the class. The pitch may receive up to 8 pts that will form part of the final assignment's grade (the Campaign Plan). The pitch will be brief (recorded, please indicate in writing if your do not wish to be recorded). More information on Blackboard. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
